speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number 4. And we are in-- at the camp of Mauritius and the life of Mauritius.

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm trying to figure out where I stopped. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] What other people-- what the people did, I think I said that. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Well, one of the most unpleasant thing in Mauritius was really malaria. And lots during the first couple of months, I think maybe at least 1/4 of all the people on Mauritius had gotten malaria at least one time. It was extremely unpleasant. And we had mosquito nets, but you still had to be outside, and the mosquitoes just got to you. And malaria is a very unpleasant illness. And I myself, I think I said it already, I had it about 13 times, which was really quite bad. So I was in and out of the hospital. And one time, I was particularly sick. I-- after-- I had a different kind of mosquito, which gave you-- or not mosquito, but a different kind of virus, which gave you the malaria. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] this was worse than the others. And after I was finished with the malaria, I got jaundice and I was in the hospital for about two months. I think I lost probably 30 pounds during that time. And I was really ill. But after that, I somehow-- it got a little bit better. They tried-- when people were really ill, so they tried to get them some recreational facilities. Not recreational, but somehow-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] now I'm lacking a word again. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Just after-- after an illness--

speaker: interviewer
text: Recovery?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. Some-- some recovery-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] helping recovery places. And I remember they sent me once to-- they had a camp of-- for [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] people who had been quite ill, which sometimes they let even young people like me go to. And I spent I think five days once in such a facility, which was helpful. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] As far as outings were concerned, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] at the beginning, there were very few outings. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I think the first year we were not allowed to go out at all. After about a year, they let you go out maybe every three, four months, for five, six hours. You had to apply and they gave you permission and then you could-- then you could go. Eventually they let you go out more often, and even for the whole day, towards the end. It was really pretty stupid, because I don't know-- again, it was one of those unnecessary things, because you really couldn't run away from Mauritius. There was no place for you to go. So why they didn't let you go out, I never understand. And it was again one of those things that the commanders certainly should have made easier for all of us. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] During-- in 1943, I believe it was, suddenly they stopped all outings without any explanation. We didn't know why, until later on we found out that there were some Japanese submarines near the island and they had sunk several vessels. And so [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] the food, which had to be imported to Mauritius, was getting scarce. And I guess the inhabitants of Mauritius tried to put it on the Jews [LAUGHS] and that they didn't have enough to eat-- not on the Jews, on the camp-- that we didn't have enough to eat. But that changed again after two, three months. And that was sort of understandable. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] The-- [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] the natives, actually at the beginning were really very, very nice to us. Later on, I think they got a little not so nice anymore, because they felt we were taking some food away from them and so on and so forth. That was also understandable. But basically, nobody did anything bad really to any of us and most people were reasonably friendly. There were about I believe 12 Palestine police with us in the camp, 9 women and-- no, it must have been more, maybe 15. There were about-- there were 5 men and-- now 14-- 5 men and 9 women who were with us all the time. And most of them were reasonably nice, except for the commander, who was really very unpleasant. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] All in all, life in the camp was not easy. We-- I was very lucky to manage to get into a hut which had a division. And one part of the hut was slated for five people. And I happened to be in this particular area. And that was much more pleasant than being in a hut with 32 other people. We were pretty crowded in that space. I remember we-- we counted. We had one locker, one bed. And the whole area was seven planks, you know, of the wooden floor. So that was sort of our home for almost five years. And everybody was pretty restless and pretty unhappy and not knowing what was going to become of us, because we were-- had been told originally that we never could go back to Palestine, that we were forever banned from there. So nobody really knew what was going to happen. The war was going on. We-- most of us did not have any letters from our parents. I-- after '42, everything stopped, because most of our parents were deported at that time. And so we had no idea what was happening. I had relatives in Palestine, and apparently my parents had at some time or other given them some money, if-- because at that time they thought, obviously, that I was going to get to Palestine, so that I had-- that I would have some money once I got there. So this way, I could get some money from my relatives from Palestine, because they could send the money to Mauritius. And my brother was in Palestine at that time. He was younger than I and he had been able to go legally on a children's certificate. And he spent the war with my relatives in-- in Palestine. But obviously, the fact that we never heard from our parents anymore, that really got to us. And we had-- I think I had forebodings all the time that I'm never going to see them again. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] In 1942, or even the end-- '40, '41, the end-- it might have been-- no, I think it was '42. In 1942, Czech young men had volunteered to go into the Czech army. And it took quite a few months before they were allowed to do so, but finally they were. And I think it was in August of '42-- somehow sounds right to me-- that about almost a hundred of them left. And there were a couple of officers who went to London. And the rest went to the Middle East, Africa. And actually, about 30 of them deserted in Palestine, which was rather unpleasant. But somehow, it could be understood. They never knew whether they could return there and they were there. And most of them eventually joined the Jewish Brigade, so it was really not because they were afraid to-- of the fighting, but because, I guess, also people in the kibbutzim talked them into it, and so they did. And the Czechs actually were quite nice. They really didn't prosecute them, so maybe they could understand it, too.

speaker: interviewer
text: Were-- were you aware of the-- the war ending at--

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yeah. We had a radio and we could listen to the BBC. And there were some newspapers. And, you know, most of us-- at least the Czech [? transcript ?] spoke some English and spoke some French, so we could get newspapers. And-- but the main thing, there was a central radio, which every time, every day, even, we had news. When there were news, we could listen to them. And so we knew exactly what was going on. I mean, I probably less than many others, because I wasn't that interested at that time yet. But we knew exactly what was happening to the-- to the war. And [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] we-- the-- the authorities, the camp authorities, I mean our authorities were trying all the time to make the British change their mind, to let us back into-- into Palestine. And they tried to convince them that they did the wrong thing. And they wrote letters to London and wrote letters all over the place. And eventually, in-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] in the beginning of-- I think in February of 1945, we got amnesty. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And so there were preparation for us to leave. But again, something interfered. First of all, it was difficult to get shipping at that time. And second, there was a big polio epidemic in Mauritius and we were quarantined for a couple of months. There were lots of people who died at that time. It was really one of the largest epidemics, I heard, in the whole world. And there were quite a few people, even from the US, came to Mauritius at that time to check-- I mean physicians or scientists-- to check out that epidemic. And we had five cases in the camp. One of them died. So we were quarantined for a couple of months. And then there were a couple of-- three, as a matter of fact-- cyclones on Mauritius, which we had never experienced during the-- it's strange-- during the five years. All of a sudden, there were three in a row. And that was-- they really devastated the island. And so again, we were delayed.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we-- we made it only after the war ended. We left Mauritius on August, I believe the 12th, 1945, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on the which was a British troop ship. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And again, it took about-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] took a little less, I think. We made it in two weeks this time. And we arrived in-- in Palestine on the 26th of August, 1945. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Yeah. That was our odyssey. What was it like, arriving in-- in Palestine?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, I must say the first year in Palestine for me was the worst year of my whole life, because I was all alone. My parents were gone. And until then, I had still hope. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] And, I don't know, it was terrible. I didn't know what to do with myself. I hadn't finished school. I had no profession. And I stayed with my relatives for a while, but, you know, that was temporary. And then I decided [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] that I might as well become a nurse, since I had worked in Mauritius. And actually-- so I went to work in Afula, at a hospital as a nurse's aide, and sort of hoping that eventually I'll find a way somehow to go into nurse's training. And then something interfered. I forgot to mention that I had-- before I left Czechoslovakia, I had had an accident. I tore the meniscus on my left knee. And so I had had surgery before I left Czechoslovakia. And somehow, the surgery-- apparently, they didn't notice that I also had torn my cruciate ligament, and they didn't fix the cruciate ligament. And my knee was never the same. And I always had trouble after that surgery. And so when I worked in the hospital as a nurse's aide, I-- very frequently, my-- my knee gave way. And so I talked to one of the doctors there, and they talked me into new surgery, and I stupidly went for it. And the surgery, they put a-- they took a piece out of my tendon and tried to make an artificial cruciate ligament out of it. But it really didn't work, so my knee has never been in good shape.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when the-- the opportunity arose so that I could really enter nurse's training, they gave us a-- gave us a-- a special arrangement had been made for some of the female soldiers in the war, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] who had, you know, served during the war and who wanted to become nurses. And they made the training, which normally lasted three years, they made it into two years, and they accepted a couple of us to this particular training. So we had to study Hebrew. We studied Hebrew like crazy. We had to pass an exam, which I passed. And then I didn't pass the physical, because of my knee. So that was that. So after that happened, I stayed in Israel for-- in Palestine for about a year and a half. And during that time, I had 17 jobs. I worked as a clerk. My English was pretty good at that time already, and I worked as a clerk in one of the military hospitals. And then I worked as clerk in one of the military camps.

speaker: interviewee
text: And then I worked as a-- [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] not as a-- helper in a restaurant, or in two restaurant. Then I worked for a little factory, which made lipsticks. I don't know what else I did, but I constantly changed jobs. And I really didn't know what to do with myself. Then finally, I-- there was a course for social workers, which I decided I might take. But it was very difficult. I had really great difficulties with the language. And I was working very hard and I didn't have enough money and it was very unpleasant. And in the meantime, I had-- I had an aunt in my hometown who was in a mixed marriage. And she had survived. She was only in Terezin for about three months. And-- and she had survived the war, obviously. And she didn't have any children, and she started writing to me.

speaker: interviewer
text: She met this guy who had gone to school with me, or that guy who had gone to school with me, and they're all studying. And so why don't you come? And we don't have any children, so we'll help you. So one day I decided I'm working so hard here, I might as well do something a little bit better. And so I decided to go back. Where? Go back where?

speaker: interviewee
text: To Czechoslovakia. That was in 1946. The end of 1946, I decided to give it a try, see what happen. We also had a house there, which I thought I might be able to get back, so that might finance my studies. And I went back to Czechoslovakia [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] on a Greek boat. I still remember. I was so seasick the whole time. It was terrible. It was in December and the sea was awful.

speaker: interviewer
text: So it took us about six weeks. I went with another guy who went back at the same time as me. And we-- it took us six weeks because the trains were not going yet and we had to stay here for two weeks and here for three weeks before a train finally went.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so we got into Prague about six weeks after we had left Palestine. And he had some relatives in Prague and I didn't know a soul in Prague. And I arrived-- we arrived on a Saturday, and I didn't have a penny in my pocket, because I had borrowed some money, you know, for trip, but that was all gone. We thought the trip would last a week, but it lasted six.

speaker: interviewer
text: So here I was in Prague on the Wenceslas Square on a Saturday, where everything is-- every Jewish organization is closed, without a penny in my pocket. And this friend of mine who came with me just said goodbye and left. And I was standing in the middle of Wenceslas Square. It was so unbelievable. And the next unbelievable thing happened. There came a guy from my hometown. All of a sudden, he was standing in front of me. Hey, what are you doing here? So that was luck. So I borrowed some money from him and took the train to my hometown. And there my-- I didn't have money to phone, you know. My-- my relatives could have probably wired me some money or something. So it was very lucky that I met him. So I came home and was very sad, obviously. I hadn't been there in, what, eight years? And nobody was there except my aunt, who I incidentally had never really been close to. But they-- they were-- they were nice to me. I can't complain. And now what's the problem? I had to finish school. So-- and I came a little too late. Used to be all sorts of courses in '45 and '46 for people like me, who were lacking a couple of years. But at that time, they had all stopped already, because all the people had already taken them, and there was nothing left. So I had to go to all the ministers, and just got myself the permission to do it on my own. And I got it, and I got all the papers. And I went-- I went to a-- first, I tried at my school in my hometown, but obviously, the German school was closed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And the Czech school, the-- the mean guy who was my Czech professor was now the director, who had hated me at that time. So when I came to the school and said, hey, I would like, you know, to be able to make my matura or baccalaureate, he said, no way. You have German schools, and you better stop doing that. They might even deport you. I'll never forget that. He told me that.

speaker: interviewer
text: So then I found a lawyer in my hometown who had a friend who was director of a school in Prague. And I was very lucky that I had those three months in the Czech school, because at that time, this German papers-- I mean papers from German school, you were really in deep trouble in-- you know, after the war. And I had those three months from the Czech school, which had my last report card. And all they always demanded was your last report card, so I could give them that last report card, which wasn't very good, but that was unimportant. I had passed.

speaker: interviewee
text: And so what they allowed me to do, which was very nice, really, was take a test in every subject, rather than go through the whole matura. So I-- there were like 13 subjects, and I had to take a test in every subject. And I studied like crazy, and I guess I did it.

speaker: interviewer
text: And people were very nice. They really helped. I mean, most professors were super-nice. I know I went there and said, hey, listen, I want to go to medical school and I just have to pass this test and can you maybe help me how to pass it? And they always some-- they-- they're lenient and they gave me suggestions and they told me what they're going-- approximately what they're going to ask me on the test.

speaker: interviewee
text: It was rough. I studied for 3 months, about 16 hours a day, day after day after day. And I passed. And in September of '47, I started medical school in Prague. And in February '48, the Communists came. And I decided I don't want to stay here, because I really didn't have anybody in Czechoslovakia and I wanted to go to-- back to Palestine. And so I decided I'm going to go to Vienna. And somehow we managed to get to Vienna. And I continued in Vienna, and finished in Vienna in '54. And by that time, I had met my husband. And we got married in 1955. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] And he was still finishing his PhD, and I didn't-- I couldn't practice in Vienna, because I didn't have Austrian citizenship. So I-- since we didn't have any money, we were very poor at that time. So I went back to Israel at that time and I did my internship in Israel. And finished medical-- he finished his thesis and got his PhD. And then I came back and then we came here. In the meantime, he had-- even though we really wanted to go to Israel. But in the meantime, he had gotten by chance somehow they-- they offered him to go to the United State without a quota, because he was a fugitive from Communism. And so he took it. He said OK, and we came here without a penny. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] That's the rest of the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: What-- what is your husband's name?

speaker: interviewee
text: My husband's name?

speaker: interviewer
text: Yes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh. Rudy.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when-- when did you arrive to the United States?

speaker: interviewee
text: We came in I think October the 23rd-- [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] either October or November, I can't remember now, in '56.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what was it like for you here?

speaker: interviewee
text: We had a relatively easy time here. We were both-- we had a pretty good education. He came at a time where engineers-- he was a chemical engineer-- well, he is a chemical engineer. And he came at the time where engineers were in great demand. And so he had a job offer very, very early. And he went to work for DuPont in Wilmington, Delaware. And I started my internship in-- we came to New York originally and I started my internship in New York. So I stayed in New York for about six months, because they didn't want to let me go until they found a replacement for me. And then I transferred to Wilmington. And we lived in Wilmington for four years. And then we came to Phoenix.

speaker: interviewer
text: We're going to stop at this point.

speaker: interviewee
text: OK.
